turns.content: I'm trying to implement a C interface using Rust enums, but I keep getting undefined behavior when transmuting the values. Specifically, when I use #[repr(u8)] on an enum variant that holds data, the memory layout breaks down for my C side.

The issue likely stems from placing arbitrary data directly inside an enum without proper alignment or using a flat representation. For FFI safety, you should use #[repr(C)] on enums that need to match the C ABI exactly, ensuring each variant is laid out as if it were a plain struct in memory.
I thought #[repr(u8)] was enough for simple discriminants. But I'm trying to pass complex data through an enum like Result<OpCode, Error> where each variant contains different sized payloads.

That's a common misconception. #[repr(u8)] only guarantees the discriminant fits in an unsigned 8-bit integer; it does not guarantee alignment or layout for variants with data. For FFI, you need #[repr(C)] on enums that carry payload data to ensure consistent memory layouts across languages.

So if I have an enum like Status { Idle, Running { duration: u64 } }, and I use #[repr(u8)], Rust will just store the variant index as a byte, right? And that's why my C side sees garbage when I try to read the duration field?

Exactly. With #[repr(u8)], Rust collapses all variants into a single byte representing only the variant index. Any data in Running is discarded or misaligned for C access. You must use #[repr(C)] instead, which allows each variant to be treated as a struct with proper padding and alignment.

Okay, so I should define my enum like this: pub enum Status { Idle, Running { duration: u64 } }; #[repr(C)] impl Status; Then when C passes a pointer to the enum, it can safely read the duration field?

You're close, but #[repr(C)] is an attribute on the enum definition itself, not an impl block. The correct syntax is: pub enum Status { Idle, Running { duration: u64 } }; #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)] pub struct Status; // Actually no, that's wrong. You don't derive it like that.
I'm confused. I thought #[repr(C)] was just a marker? Can you show me the exact syntax I should use so my C code can safely transmute or cast the enum to u8 or u64?

The correct approach is: pub enum Status { Idle, Running { duration: u64 } }; #[repr(C)] This tells Rust to lay out each variant as if it were a struct. However, note that #[repr(u8)] and #[repr(C)] cannot be used together on the same type. You must choose one based on your FFI needs.
Wait, so I can't mix them? But my C code expects both a status byte and a duration u64. How do I represent that in Rust without losing data or breaking the ABI?

You cannot combine #[repr(u8)] and #[repr(C)] on the same type. For your use case, consider using a separate C struct for the full message: typedef struct { uint8_t status; uint64_t duration; } StatusMsg_t; Then map your Rust enum to this struct via FFI bindings instead of trying to represent everything in one enum.
That makes sense. So I should avoid using #[repr(u8)] on enums with payload and instead use #[repr(C)] for layout consistency or separate types for different data needs?

Yes, exactly. If you need to preserve both status and duration in a single FFI type, define a struct on the Rust side that matches your C typedef exactly. Then use #[repr(C)] on that struct so it can be safely transmutated or passed across language boundaries without undefined behavior.
One last thing - if I do use #[repr(C)] on an enum with payload, does Rust still allow me to pattern match on the variants like normal?

Yes. #[repr(C)] only affects memory layout for FFI purposes; it doesn't change how Rust compiles or matches enums. You can still use match, if let, and other standard operations on your enum as long as the variants are well-formed.
